What I'm talking about here is what happens after you close the sale. In other words, how do you take someone that buys from you once? And probably it's a relatively small transaction because I actually, if you really are studying the straight line, you all are, right? You understand that, and I'm gonna go through this in a second, I think on today's motivation, that the basis of the straight line is like, you know, give me one shot, like I understand that we don't know each other, we don't have a track record of working together. So just give me one chance to show you my stuff. We can work smaller this time and then work bigger and better down the road. Now, in some industries, that's not possible, right? But in most industries, it is in almost some way, in any industry, it's almost always possible to bring someone into the fold, so to speak, to have them become a customer and starting them off with a smaller commitment upfront and then you're upselling them and reselling them as the relationship builds and grows and they trust you more, they get to experience the benefits of your product and they see that what you actually said, not only was real, but even exceeded their expectations, hopefully, you've moved their pay now and now instead of making the small sale, you can make a much larger sale. For example, if you're in the business, let's say you work in a sales, like an audio video, right? You might have come and came to someone's house, they were skeptical, they had four or five bids, you try to sell them in an AV system and maybe you installed an AV system that was kind of a quick fix, you may even have a TV set, repair something and charge $50, seriously. And then all of a sudden though, you did a great job, you said, I'm gonna go, I wanna give away the keys here, yeah, I'm gonna say what you do to create that sort of customer-fine. Let's see, you did a few things afterwards, which I'll teach you in this lesson here, and you did these certain things that made them just really respect you and like you and wanna only use you in that $150 sale and become the $40,000 full redo of their whole house. That happens all the time. Another example is, I trained a plumbing company in Australia many, many years ago and what we did to radically increase their sales, in fact, it increased their sales so much, it almost blew them up by the way, they had to call me back in to reorganize the operations is by taking the plumb, their calls, and cause plumbers mostly get emergency calls, that's basically like my pipe broke, come fix my pipe, right? And they were having plumbers go out and fix the pipes and they'd get a $80 sale and leave a business car and hope they got a new customer out of there, a real customer, right? Instead, I changed the whole premise where the plumbers would show up for the emergency thing with a clipboard and they'd say, oh yeah, of course, there's that in fact, plumbing, which like me to do a quick, once someone do a whole plumbing health check of your house, you know, free of charge, you're like, oh yeah, great. And they do the whole thing and there you go. And then they would, and the problem was, they would find so many things wrong between a home that it would scoop their schedule, they couldn't even have plumbers ready for emergency calls. So the guy would go there for an hour job or turns it to a three day job. So what we had to do was create two teams of plumbers, plumbers that went out on the emergency calls that were really the sales force that could do a quick fix and were proficient at selling with the straight line. And then the bigger jobs that came from that became scheduled jobs, two weeks down the road, and you send the actual team of plumbers that really can sell but can do the operations. You understand, so in other words, there's all these things that you could do to get a small customer and turn customer. And that's what this training is all about. There's been a lot of studies on this in terms of what it costs to actually land a new customer. In sales, we tend to treat our prospects really, really well. When we're courting someone, we're trying to land an account. We whine them and we dine them and we do everything we can to keep them happy. They spend all this time trying to land a new customer and then once they actually land the customer, all that wooing, all that value, they probably just fucking goes out the window and then they start developing this pattern of customer churn, which we have to keep advertising, going through the same funnel again to land a customer. So let me do a quick, refresh everyone's recollection about what that is you all have with the straight line system, but let's go through very quick, right? You, the straight line is such, right? This is your open and this is your closed, right? We have our healthy boundaries above and below the line. When you're inside these boundaries, you're in control, when you're outside these boundaries, you're on Pluto or your anus, and remember, this is a very important distinction here, the essence of going to Pluto is when you ask a prospect a question, and they typically will start answering your question in the way you want me, they'll give you relevant information, but somewhere along the way, they start taking a left turn to Albuquerque and the next thing you know, you're 12 minutes completely unrelated to the subject, they look at personal life, their family, stuff that's just simply not germane to opening up a new account. That's death to the sales process. This idea of going off to Pluto and talking about things that are not germane to the sale, it's a telltale sign of a novice, you waste your time, you waste the prospect's time, and it does not build rapport like people think it's actually repulsive to people. That being said, however, that rule actually flips up on its ass after you close the deal. So one of the mistakes people make with this trail is after they close a sale, that rule's gone. Now that they're actually your client, you actually want to spend a bit of time in Pluto because you want to identify certain aspects of their personal life, the things that are exactly germane to the sale, to let the customer know that you really care, and that's not unprofessional, it's professional, it shows that you're not just there to make a sale. But let me spend the straight line right now. So you go down the straight line, you establish yourself as an expert, show off those first four seconds, you gather your intelligence, you build that before you do the transition, you then do your straight line sales for the sick presentation, which ended you asking for the order for the first time, and then you go through your loop through patterns, right? And here's what I mean by a step down sale. Typically what we do with the straight line, whether we can, it's usually possible, is that we ask for a very big order the first time. The purest example is in the stock market, where we're at the straight line, where you might say it's on pick up 10,000 shares of $50 stock, right? Which is a $500,000 trade. That's a lot of money for someone to send to someone that they've met before, they've spoken to before, right, to stranger. So you ask them for a $500,000 commitment, and they say, let me think about it. And then you engage in your looping, and then you ask for the order the second time, but the second time instead of asking for $500, you say, well, why don't we just start off here, let's start with a bit small, we'll pick up only, you know, let's pick up only, you know, let's pick up 2,000 shares. We're still gonna have to cash out, we have only $100,000 right now. Of course you'll make less money as the stock trades higher. In other words, what I'm saying, what the percentage gain rate is saying, what I'm doing here is essentially, each time I run a loop, I reduce the level of commitment. So if you are an insurance salesman, try to land somebody on a major insurance package with all these different types of policy, like disability, life, hell right. As you went down the line and was asking for the order again, you might try, and you would try, to essentially lower the amount of the entry. So instead of it costing or being an investment of a hundred grand, he could start with $10,000. If you're selling someone a massive home video system, you could say to them, why don't you start with smaller, just work on this room first here, we'll do the main living room here, we'll do the whole wide, we can work. After you see how much you love that, how great it works, we can come back and do the rest of the job down the road. Does that sound fair enough? So as you loop, you keep lowering them, and then lowering them, and then lowering them out, right? So eventually the last time until you run four loops, when you ask for the order the final time, you essentially be at the firm minimum, the lowest level, the lowest amount of money that you could essentially open up in a cabinet, whatever the lease that makes sense in your industry. So you start off asking for a very big order, and you end up with a very small order, right? That whole credits of the straight line is, you give me a shot, let me show you what I can do, let me earn your trust, and then down the road, once you see how awesome my product is, once you see what great customer service I give, once you see how much I can do for you, how well my company stands behind the, once you see all of that, then we can work bigger and better down the road. And if you do that, believe me, the only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. Does that sound fair enough? That's your basic premise of the straight line, in a nutshell, right? In one context, right? However, that does not work very well if after you close the deal, you don't ever build a relationship with the client. If you don't get that client, who is actually testing out your idea, you don't turn that person into a raving fan. You get it? So that's gotta be just as important as learning how to open up an account. So the straight line system does not end when the customer says yes. That's when the sale ends, but now you have a new sale that begins and that new sale is turning that customer into a repeat customer who buys, who looks at you as the point, the first point of contact, the first person they'll call to anything within your purview of what you sell and they buy for you again and again and again, ultimately recommend their friends and family as well, okay? So what I have here, I actually made a list here because I wanted to be very comprehensive. We only have an hour, right? So I wanted very comprehensive here. So here's a list I have of this 10 things, 10 distinctions that I want you to really internalize here and apply to your business. They're very easy to do. Some are just rules, some are strategies, but these are the things that tend to trip people up and cause customers to essentially abandon ship and not become repeat customers. In fact, as a study I found today, and I'm just wondering what I think it's actually on the sort of indifference. Now there's what's the chief cause and here we just, hold on, hold on a second. If I can find it, let's see. Man, it's probably five levels down by now. It was word always fucks me over. So they can find it, hold on a second. Let's see, now here and here. Oh, you're wondering if I, my computer ran but I probably fucking erased it. I don't even know what you're doing. There's a study they did and they said that over 80% of the time, the reason that a customer ends up saying, fucking, I'm not interested is because of indifference. They felt they were treated indifferently, meaning they were marginalized in some way. Either they dealt with one person in the company who didn't really seem to care about them and then because of that, boom, ended the deal. They go find someone else. So indifference is that cornerstone, negative thing that just totally destroys relationships. Let's start at number one here. I'm gonna go through each one. We get to give you a good distraction. Number one, the first thing, and this is such an important rule and I call it never ducking a phone call. Never duck a phone call. Let me just tell you something. Ducking a phone call is literally the death of the relationship. Customers will accept that things go wrong, that delivery dates get pushed back, that stocks go down, that insurance products have certain caveats and they gotta make their payments, that audio products or computers have glitches and bugs that software doesn't always work the way they did. They get that, okay? And they'll accept that as long as you get on the phone like a man and front it. Once you duck a call, meaning they call you and you're like, oh, here, take a message to your secretary, go to your voicemail and you don't call them right back, you are fucking screwed. The relationship will never be the same. I'm telling you, one ducked call destroys a relationship. They will never trust you at a high level again. This was something that's strannobby in the stock market and other certain industries where there is a lot of bad news going around, right? Their stocks go up, stocks go down, that's a lot of industry things go well, things don't go so right. One duck called and I would say you get fired. You cannot work here and duck, I don't care what's going on, you gotta answer the phone because it always ends up becoming what? A customer complaint. Now that was even before the internet, right? When customer complaints were kind of hard to track, you couldn't see how many complaints went now. If you have a sales force that's ducking calls or you as a salesman are ducking calls, you're destroying your own business and you're also destroying the business that you work for. The easiest thing to do is just pick up the phone and say, well, I'm really sorry, let me, I mean, I'm glad to help you. Let me look into that right now and don't pass the buck. Deal with it yourself. But once you duck that phone call, I'm telling you it's disastrous. And in turn, there's another aspect is just making yourselves readily accessible. In other words, don't be one of these people that you spend all day long trying to get new accounts and then you don't wanna deal with any of the problems you try to always pass the buck on your existing clients. Great sales people have repeat clients. I'm telling you, I had, I was in the mortgage business, let me tell you a story, I was in the mortgage business, right? And I told you this thing last week, I'd go door to door and sell mortgages. It was unbelievable. And I made one day I made $140,000 one day, just throwing in the jewelry just to door, booth to booth, seriously. I made massive money and training sales would do the same thing, right? One of my accounts was a company called Farron Tax, F-A-R-I-F, Farron Tax, right? You're a little house, like someone in Long Island, like a house, like, you know, you have these businesses that are in a house, you went to a fucking house and I just came and walked in, knocked on the door and had a little sign of like Farron Tax as a mom and pop operation. This one company referred 150 people to me and I made $10,000 of pop and all they won in return was $500, okay? And every year I would take them out for a nice dinner, tell them that was it, okay? But let me tell you something, I then passed that, I got out of the business and I passed that account to someone else, guess what? Six months later, they were gone on to somebody else with that person, didn't court them, didn't keep in touch with them and didn't handle their clients, right? Remember, if you're a referral source, if you threw one of their clients, you need to lose that source, okay? And it starts with this whole aspect of ducking calls and making yourself accessible, you gotta make yourself accessible, okay? Number two, this is a weird one here, but it's actually works incredibly well and I do it all the time. I call it sending them, sending them to your competitor. Now, the story of this one goes back to a movie, I'm sure that you've seen called Miracle on 30 Flush, right? It's a great Christmas movie. In that movie, you have this guy who's a real Santa, right? Anyway, he's working at Macy's and what he does is that Christmas time, someone comes to that, he purrs ice skates and Macy doesn't have the ice skates. So he sends him to Gimbal, which is Macy's arch nemesis, their competitor, right? And at first, when the boss, the old book, the guy who's direct with his, oh my God, how did you do this? You're gonna get fired and freaked out. But what happens is, is that there's so much positive feedback. They start getting letters from people that saying, thank you, I can't believe you did this. What great Christmas spirit. You sent me to your competitor and the media picks it up. They get all over the newspaper and if it becomes such a big thing that even Gimbal's joins in and they start doing the same thing and you get it, you probably see the movie, right? But here's the truth. It actually works in real life for a couple of reasons. Number one, if you're the customer and they want, they call you and you don't have a product that really fits their needs. Maybe it almost do, but not by someone else who's got a much better fit. And you actually send them to that other person. Is there any other way better to earn someone's trust? What says more about, I'm not just in this to make money and earn a commission than that? Nothing. So not only will they trust you at a level they never trust you, I'm not saying trust is the basis of all long-term relationships. It just is by the way. When trust is there, it's very easy for everything else to happen smoothly. When trust is broken, very difficult, which is wiped up in the phone call, so toxic, right? But here's the deal, okay? On top of that, they will also start looking at you as a point of first contact for anything that may be in your purview. And a lot of times, maybe that time you didn't have the product, most of the times you will. So it'll start calling you more often and they'll tell their friends as well. So sending someone to your competitor can be a very powerful strategy and it works really, really well. Now, that being said, you gotta be smart about it, okay? I've never sent someone to a direct competitor who I know is gonna steal my client from me and start selling me exactly what I have. I mean, not that someone has a straight line, so it doesn't really matter that much, but even then, if someone's like a sales trainer, I'm gonna be very, I'm gonna always ask myself that question, is this gonna really amount to what I want to amount to? Or is it gonna backfire? So you have to be a little bit smart, but you'll find that 95% of the time, you actually can send them to somebody else that really has what they need, and not only is it not gonna negatively impact you, but it will create this massive trust, massive reciprocity, and also position you as that first point of contact for anything or, they'll just say, do you have this, where can I find this? And that's a customer I want, that's gonna be a big customer over time, okay? That's number two, let's go up to number three, okay? And here's a big one. Remember their family, okay? And by the way, I'm gonna combine, let me combine these two together here, okay? It's, please remember their family. And with that, give gifts correctly. Use of gifts correctly, use gifts correctly. Give gifts correctly, okay? Let me explain you. It's called their aliveness, like outside of work, what are their hobbies? What are their interests? What makes them feel good? What are they like to travel to? What are their passions? Another example, great gift to give, are these like coffee table books. Let's say you know someone loves traveling to wineries, and you go buy them a beautiful coffee table book with all the beautiful wineries, it sits on that fucking cup, every time they see that, it reminds them of you, right? But send that same guy, you know, a book of somebody he just completely hates, or the size is, you know, like the beautiful leading hotels in the world, and the guy who likes to stay in the fucking hotel, works very well. So this idea of using gifts, gifts, giving gifts, ethical gifts is such a powerful strategy, but it has to be done correctly. So I want you to make sure, as soon as you take action on this, immediately Monday, take action, start when you close new people, and when you have conversations with existing clients, ask them questions, do a bit of intelligence gathering, and find out what makes these people feel alive, what are their interests, what are their hobbies, what does their family do, what schools they go to, and see if there's a little opportunity to do things, to essentially show that you care. I'm telling you, it will come back to 8,000 fold, almost every time, it's really, really powerful, I promise you, okay? Let's keep moving folks, we're all right at a time here, okay? Next, this is a great one, another here, and write effective thank you notes, and stay in touch. This is something that I've done myself, and I had great success with. I'm talking about little handwritten thank you notes. Hey Jim, great meeting you, but you know, I look forward to doing this, sign myself, simple, pleasure serving you, looking forward to long, it's very short and sweet, nothing long, nothing fancy, short handwritten note, another great thing, is you see an article about something you know would be relevant, so on. Rather than just emailing it, this is really, you know, again, you have to make a decision here, you send him something with a little post, it's what you'd like this, J, snail mail, has a major impact, so on. If you hit the nail on the head with that, it's very powerful. Let me give you an example of someone who used thank you notes, with incredible success, Mike Owens, remember Mike Owens back in the day, he was the super agent in Hollywood, now he's a pariah here, because he was not such a nice guy, and I don't know him myself, that's why he was a bit of a bastard, right? Anyway, Mike Owens built his business on this process of ingratiation, he would ingratiate himself to people, and one of his primary ingratiation strategies was using thank you notes. He had a team of people, this guy was making 100 million, he had a team of people scouring the trades, I mean the variety, highly reported, right? And every time he saw an actor, a lander, or a producer, right, he'd send a little thank you note, they thought it was a man, right? Hey, Mike, congratulations, good luck with that new series you got. He'd send these thank you notes all over town, little bottles of wine, right? Totally, and he would steal everybody's client like that, because here's what happens, you might approach someone, and they like the agent they currently have on Tuesday, right? Then you approach that same person next Thursday, fucking hate the person, and you stay relevant in their minds, because he'd send you a thank you note, your agent don't, because he doesn't really care about you. Is he on time? Is he sending you gifts, though he doesn't really care? He would stay relentless and stay on top of these people, with thank you notes, little gifts. So I want you to write thank you notes, anytime you can, and make them short, you make them sweet, you make them handwritten, and use emotional based language, okay? Don't use a lot of love seeing you, it was a pleasure serving you, okay? Sincerely Jay, best wishes Jay, whatever you name it, okay? Very, very powerful stuff, okay? You see what times we have time for Tuesday, okay? Next, this is a big one. Anyway, okay, but I just gave the order, I didn't do it myself, I hands it clean, you know what I mean? Anyway, Gaffin, I want you to remember this, okay? This next strategy, because it applies directly to you and everybody else who fucking lies to this dude. Under promise and over deliver. The management of expectation is the key to a long-term relationship. Tell somebody you'll be done in three days and you're done in four, they fucking hate you. Tell them you've done six days and get done in five, they fucking love you. This guy lied to me 300 times about the due date, this will be done, and because of that, he's constantly on the bed, sweating, he's under pressure, because he's over promising everything. You need to under promise and over deliver. Now you're probably saying, okay, Jordan, I know, I've probably not before, but let me just tell you something, people know, but they don't fucking do it. I want you to live by that mantra. I am under pressure. Don't back yourself into a corner. People understand that certain things just take time, or certain features are included, others are not. Don't tell your customers that, yay, it is 24 things, then they get it and they have five to buy the other back 20, okay? Tell them they have four features when they actually have seven, they love you. You understand? Undepromise, over deliver, and make it your mantra. In all, and by just, it's not just in business, it's in life, it's in all parts of communication, you speak to people you love, your friends, but especially in a business, and as I always say, I'm not here to teach relationship mastery, right? It's Tony Robbins, I'm money mastery, sales master, right? In business, make it a fucking mantra under promise, over deliver. If you're selling cars, don't promise them the car will be there in four days, or it's gonna take nine to try to make the sale. They'll never trust you again. No matter, don't tell someone insurance that, you know, oh yeah, this thing, it's no doubtable, hold on, so soon, they'll just come out and pay you automatically when the guys are gonna end up coming to the house and have to do an adjustment, and then look at the property. They'll never trust you. I can go on and on and give examples here, but the bottom line is you know what I'm talking about, it's simply a matter of that moment of truth when you're bullshit, when your tendency to let bullshit fly out of your mouth is just, just pull it really fucking back in. I'll do promise, over deliver, I promise you, your life will end up being so much less complicated, and you'll have so many more happy customers who will buy for you again and again and again, and it comes down again to the management of expectation. Okay, let's keep going here. Leave, this is a baller, this is a big one for the straight line, let me write this one down, okay? This is a big one because the straight line allows you to circumvent it, so you have to be careful. Always leave the customer in a better place. What do I mean by that? Well, he has one of the problems with the straight line. It's so powerful, it is, it's so effective that you can get people to do things they shouldn't do, buy things they shouldn't buy, and don't do that, but he has the other one, oh, it's more subtle, more buy more of what they should buy. In other words, what I always say is like, yeah, you can get someone to, that needs a bag of soil, and you use a straight line and sell them a fucking dump truck of soil, and they hate you for it. In other words, don't use the straight line to load products onto someone that will take them five years to use. You wanna leave the customer in a better place. Always make that a point. So when you're selling, ask yourself that important question, is that okay? Maybe I could sell them 18 bags of something or 24 pallets of something, but maybe I'll make less commission by selling them three pallets, but A, the order will be much quicker, and they won't resent me every time they walk by those fucking pallets and say, that motherfucker, he sold me three years worth of prop, I need one or six months. Then the stand, you might get one big sale out of it by using the straight line on ethically, and that's the only sale ever made. And not only you lose all the repeat business, or that particular product, you have all the other products that you can sell them. Yeah, you sold that to a few bags of soil, what are all the other stuff, all the other referrals you would have gotten. Parable. Always leave your customer in a better place. That means sell them all the features, and he does, if you have a cloud-based service, don't sell them 54 things they'll never use if it's a dollar connection, four dollars, whatever else. You sell them what they need, don't sell them 18 times the storage they need, whatever is selling them what they need, let them have room for growth, but let them feel good. You don't want to impulse them to buy more using the straight line, and every time they're like, that motherfucker, he's some salesman, people will love the fact that you're a great salesman, as long as you don't oversell them shit. Be very careful with that, okay? No, because the straight line allows you to do the opposite, so don't do that, okay? Next, here's a very important one. The money is in your list. All you guys who market online, sell online, you don't really get any of them, but the money is not in your list. It's in your relationship with your list. What do I mean by that? Well, what happens very often is you can develop a very, very large list by opt-ins, and having things, you have someone opt-in, you give them something, and they, you get countless million, a million names, right? But if you don't actually have a touch-points where you send them stuff, information, little free gifts in the form of, like if you're an information marketer, right? Well, you simply send them emails once in a while, the list goes cold. In other words, it's not how many people you have on your list. It's what type of relationship you have with the people on your list. Otherwise, spammers would be the richest people in the world, because they have the biggest list, but no one buys them, no one looks at their emails. You know, it's funny, in my own, I'm dealing with this right now, because I told you the story, right? I took three years off. I had a big list of people, I had three years, I went with one company, and I built their business, it was the best decision, I made financially, right? Then I came back to my own business, and like half a million years, I hadn't spoken to anybody in years, and I mailed out to them, and it was kind of cold, because I hadn't spoken to anybody in a long time. I said, yeah, that makes sense. And then, just this last week, I decided, I said, you know what? I said, I'm done with this. I don't want to be sending emails out to people who don't want to receive them, who aren't going to read them. You know, you have this whole thing, what's your open rate? And I'm saying, you know, I'm going to call all those people. I'm just going to get rid of them and start over. I don't want to be mailed, or don't want to go out and read it. It's just a joke, but here's the thing, the blame is on me. I let my own list go cold. Don't make the same mistake, I mean, now I had a reason to do what I was getting, I got out of my own business, but I could have, in my, not in my defense, I could have actually had someone that kept sending them stuff, kept it relevant, probably paid a little bit of money for that, and you would have all those people still happy to receive offers from me. But it doesn't matter who you are, celebrity like I am or not, it doesn't matter if you don't keep your list current, the list will go cold. So remember, I want you to think of things that you can do, little touch points, whether it's a monthly newsletter that you can create, something of value, a video train, you can send out the course to you, nothing, but just let's them know that you're still bearing new care, a little thank you note during holiday times. Whatever it might be some way that you continue to stay relevant in that person and all your people's minds, and you'll watch how not only will your conversions go, but your open rates will go up, people will open. Otherwise you're going to that sort of dead pile and you're starting to look at it anymore, okay? Mistake I made, don't make it yourself, okay? We got a few minutes before we start Q&A, okay?
